results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Warren E. Falconer was a prominent chemist and telecommunications engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) during the 1960s and 1970s. He earned his BSc and MSc degrees in chemistry from the University of Manitoba and a Doctor of Science (PhD) in chemistry from the University of Edinburgh. His early technical work focused on physical chemistry, specifically noble gas chemistry, fluorine chemistry, and molecular spectroscopy. His research frequently involved ultraviolet (UV) photolysis and the study of vacuum ultraviolet and photoelectron spectra, aligning with his recorded technical fields of 'Chemistry, Light, and Ultraviolet.' Based in locations such as Morristown and Murray Hill, New Jersey, he transitioned into leadership roles within Bell Labs, eventually serving as the Executive Director of the Transmission Systems Engineering Division and Director of the Transmission Facility Planning Center. He was an active member of the scientific community, serving as a member of the IEEE, the American Physical Society, and the Chemical Institute of Canada. He also chaired the Committee on Science for the National Research Council and participated in high-level advisory panels such as the Waste Isolation Systems Panel. Dr. Falconer is also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which facilitated collaborations between Bell Labs technical staff and the artistic community.
